---
pagetitle: "Programming with AI"
---

# Programming with AI {layout="title" data-gp-kicker="WORKSHOP / BAMBERG" data-gp-section="Opening"}

<div class="gp-title-above">Workshop / Session 3</div>
<div class="gp-code">PROGRAMMING</div>
<div class="gp-title-below">vibe coding, agents, software hygiene</div>
<div class="gp-accent-bar" data-gp-underline="tag" aria-hidden="true"></div>

::: {.gp-subtitle}
<span>Thursday, March 19, 2026 · 13:00-15:30</span>
:::

::: {.gp-meta-grid}
::: {.gp-block}
<span class="gp-label">Thu</span> March 19, 2026 · 13:00-15:30
:::
::: {.gp-block}
<span class="gp-label">Part</span> Session 3 of 3
:::
::: {.gp-block}
<span class="gp-label">Focus</span> Vibe coding, debugging, tooling, projects
:::
:::


# Schedule {layout="table" data-gp-kicker="Agenda / 01" data-gp-section="Plan"}

| Day | Time | Session | Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wed, March 18, 2026 | 14:00-16:30 | Introductory Session | Overview of useful AI tools for research work |
| Thu, March 19, 2026 | 09:30-12:00 | Text Analysis | Notebook-based text analysis with AI support |
| Thu, March 19, 2026 | 13:00-15:30 | Programming | Prompting, debugging, and notebook-based coding with AI support |


# Preparation {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Setup / 02" data-gp-section="Plan"}

* GitHub account.
* Browser plus an editor with AI support: ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, VS Code + extension, Codex / Claude Code GUI, etc.
* The shared notebook opens in [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/MNoichl/workshop_AI_bamberg/blob/main/notebooks/workshop_notebook.ipynb).
* Optional but useful: a local Python environment for the non-notebook parts.


# Plan: Programming with AI {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Scope" data-gp-section="Plan"}

* What "vibe coding" captures
* Where coding assistants are genuinely good
* Where they fail in research code
* Tool use, agents, and the command line
* Verification, privacy, backups, and cost limits
* Project vignettes you can finish in about 90 minutes


# Programming with AI {layout="divider" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Thu, March 19" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

Vibe coding, debugging, workflows


# Part 2: Coding with AI {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Why Now" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Coding with AI is one of the highest-leverage research uses of LLMs.
* Strong for boilerplate, wrappers, glue code, refactors, tests, and debugging plans.
* Also good for turning notebook chaos into reusable modules.
* The real promise is not only faster code.
* The real promise is better scientific software hygiene.


# Vibe coding {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Mode" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Describe intent in natural language.
* Let the model draft code.
* Iterate by steering, not by writing every line yourself.
* Powerful for prototypes, throwaway scripts, quick visualizations, and first versions.
* But for research code, pure vibe coding is not enough.
* Scientific code has to survive reruns, edge cases, collaborator handoff, reviewer scrutiny, and changing environments.


# Vibe for exploration. Engineer for anything you may publish or rely on. {layout="claim" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Rule" data-gp-section="Session 3"}


# A huge opportunity, and a huge trap {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Stakes" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* AI can make tests, type hints, docstrings, CLIs, packaging, and README files much easier to add.
* It also makes it easier to generate plausible nonsense, wrong abstractions, broken environments, and fake confidence.
* Benchmarks make coding agents look strong.
* Real workflows are more mixed.
* The lesson is not "AI coding works" or "AI coding fails".
* It depends on task, context, codebase familiarity, and verification setup.


# Science as amateur software development {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Framing" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* A lot of modern research is software work done by people who were not mainly trained as software engineers.
* AI does not remove that problem.
* It can help patch some of the skill and time gap.
* It is especially good for the boring professionalization layer that research teams postpone.
* Use AI not only to write analysis code, but to improve the workflow around the code.


# All those annoying little things {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Hygiene" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Commits and commit messages.
* Docstrings.
* Virtual environments.
* Packaging.
* Tests, linting, type hints, CI, and README files.
* These are often the highest-return uses.


# From text generator to tool-using coding environment {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Tool Use" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* A coding assistant becomes much more useful once it can read files, search a codebase, run tests, inspect logs, and use git.
* This changes the system from a text generator into a tool-using environment.
* It also changes the failure mode.
* The problem is no longer only "wrong answer".
* It can also be "wrong action".
* So permissions, sandboxes, and review points matter.


# Prompt engineering is over, sort of {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Prompting" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* The era of "magic spell" prompts is mostly over.
* For coding, the best prompts now look like a bug report, a task ticket, a miniature spec, or a test case.
* What helps most: relevant files, constraints, expected output, verification steps, and explicit stop conditions.
* Better than "make no mistakes":
* `run the tests after each meaningful change`
* `if a command fails, show me the failure and stop`
* `do not claim success unless the checks passed`


# Talk to the thing! {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Interface" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Natural language is now a real programming interface.
* For exploratory tasks, talking or dictating intent can be genuinely efficient.
* It works best when grounded in artifacts: files, diffs, tests, screenshots, and expected outputs.
* Good interaction pattern: say what you want, point at the relevant files, say how success will be checked, ask for the smallest robust change first.


# For scientific work: tell it to fail loudly {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Verification" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Ask the model to stop on failed tests.
* Surface stack traces and preserve stderr.
* List assumptions and uncertainty.
* Avoid silent fallbacks.
* Avoid inventing results.
* In scientific code, a quiet wrong answer is usually worse than a loud failure.


# Counterintuitively, they are lazy {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Steerability" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Models often do the minimum strongly implied by the prompt and the harness.
* If you want exhaustive behavior, ask for exhaustive behavior.
* If you want it to inspect every file matching a pattern, say so.
* If you want a debugging log, require one.
* A lot of apparent laziness is really under-specification.


# Chatbots, agents {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Systems" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* A chatbot mostly answers turns in a conversation.
* An agent acts toward a goal across multiple steps, usually with tools, state, and stop conditions.
* For coding, the simplest useful agent often looks like: model, repo access, shell or test runner, docs access, a budget, and a clear done condition.
* You usually do not need a baroque multi-agent architecture.
* Simple, composable setups often work best.


# Safety, privacy, backups {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Safety" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Before you let an AI tool rewrite, refactor, or batch-process anything important: commit or copy the current state.
* Work on a branch.
* Snapshot data if needed.
* Keep an off-site backup for anything irreplaceable.
* Also know your privacy boundary: what leaves your machine, what gets logged, and which tools can see which files.


# Set spending limits {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Cost" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Monthly budgets.
* Notification thresholds.
* Project-level limits.
* Model allowlists.
* Usage dashboards and alerts.
* Good default pattern: cheap model first, expensive model only when needed.


# A practical coding workflow for scientists {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Workflow" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Start in chat when the task is fuzzy.
* Move to an IDE or CLI agent when the task touches real files.
* Give the model the traceback, the environment spec, the expected output, and the test command.
* Ask for a minimal reproduction, the smallest robust patch, a regression check, and a commit message.
* Then review the diff like you would review a junior collaborator's work.
* Fast is good. Auditable is better.


# Project vignettes {layout="divider" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Exercises" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

Little projects you can finish in about 90 minutes


# Vignette 01: Personal website via GitHub Pages {layout="bullets" .gp-vignette data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Vignette 01" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

::: {.gp-vignette-shell}
:::: {.gp-vignette-meta}
::: {.gp-vignette-cell}
<span class="gp-label">Project</span>
`personal-site`
:::
::: {.gp-vignette-cell}
<span class="gp-label">Time</span>
`90 MIN`
:::
::: {.gp-vignette-cell}
<span class="gp-label">Mode</span>
`VIBE CODING`
:::
::: {.gp-vignette-cell}
<span class="gp-label">Output</span>
`LIVE GITHUB PAGES SITE`
:::
::::

::: {.gp-vignette-scroll}
::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Goal</div>

Build and publish a simple personal website on GitHub Pages without hand-writing much HTML and CSS.
:::

::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">What counts as done</div>

<ul class="gp-vignette-checks">
<li>A public repo with a working GitHub Pages deployment.</li>
<li>A homepage, an about section, a projects section, and a contact section.</li>
<li>A layout that works on desktop and mobile.</li>
<li>A short README explaining what to edit later.</li>
</ul>
:::

::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Ten prompts</div>

<ol class="gp-vignette-prompts">
<li>Create a minimal GitHub Pages project for a personal academic website with Home, About, Projects, and Contact. Use static HTML, CSS, and JS only.</li>
<li>Ask me six short questions about who I am, what I work on, and what should appear on the site. Then draft the site copy from my answers.</li>
<li>Create a clean file structure with <code>index.html</code>, <code>assets/site.css</code>, <code>assets/site.js</code>, and a short <code>README.md</code>.</li>
<li>Design the site in a clear visual direction instead of a default template. Make it responsive and easy to scan.</li>
<li>Add a projects section driven by a small local JSON file so I can update entries later without rewriting the page.</li>
<li>Add a contact block with email, GitHub, and one more link placeholder. Include a button for a CV or profile PDF even if the file is missing for now.</li>
<li>Set up GitHub Pages deployment on push to <code>main</code>. Explain exactly what repository setting I need to change if anything is missing.</li>
<li>Audit the site for broken links, missing alt text, mobile layout problems, and missing metadata. Fix what you can automatically.</li>
<li>Now simplify. Remove anything ornamental that makes the site harder to maintain, and keep the design intentional.</li>
<li>Write a launch checklist with the five things I should replace before I send this link to other people.</li>
</ol>
:::

::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Why this is a good vignette</div>

<ul class="gp-vignette-checks">
<li>Small enough to finish.</li>
<li>Concrete artifact at the end.</li>
<li>Real deployment step.</li>
<li>Forces you to verify, not just generate.</li>
</ul>
:::
:::
:::

<!--
Future vignette template:

# Vignette NN: Project title {layout="bullets" .gp-vignette data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Vignette NN" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

Copy the vignette shell above.
Keep one concrete output, one verification loop, and one deployment or export step.
-->


# Materials {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Links / 03" data-gp-section="Material"}

* Website: [Workshop overview](../)
* Slides: [Introductory session deck](../slides/)
* Slides: [Programming deck](../slides/programming.html)
* Notebook: [Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/MNoichl/workshop_AI_bamberg/blob/main/notebooks/workshop_notebook.ipynb)


# Room and contact {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Info / 04" data-gp-section="Material"}

* Instructor: Max Noichl
* Room: likely BAGSS seminar room
* Version date: March 18, 2026
